fact: January 13 is exactly 13 days into the new year - spooky coincidence or cosmic joke?
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many animals are deep in their winter sleep, dreaming away the coldest weeks of the year.
The sun rises later and sets earlier around this date than almost any other time of year in northern places - making days feel super short and cozy.
January 13 falls smack in the middle of Capricorn season, when the sea-goat constellation rules the zodiac with its stubborn, mountain-climbing energy.
In Australia and other southern places, this date hits right in the peak of summer - when cicadas buzz loudest and ice cream trucks work overtime.
Tree sap moves slowest around this time in cold regions, making maple trees practically frozen solid until spring wakes them up.
The moon phase on January 13 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it shines extra bright against the crisp winter air.
January 13 sits about 3 weeks after the winter solstice, meaning daylight is finally starting to creep back minute by minute in the north.
Penguins in Antarctica are typically busy raising their chicks around this date, huddling together in massive, waddling groups to stay warm.
Snow crystals form their most perfect six-sided shapes during the deep cold that hits around mid-January in snowy places.
This date marks when many northern lakes and ponds reach their thickest ice - perfect for skating but terrible for any fish trying to get some fresh air.
January 13 is roughly when winter storms pack their biggest punch in North America, dumping the heaviest snow and strongest winds.
